macro_segments.title: Introduction to the Mishnah: Carrying on Yom Tov and the Beis Shammai–Beis Hillel Debate

micro_segments.title: Overview of the shift from Muktzah to biblical aspects of Yom Tov
The Mishnah's cases: carrying a minor, lulav, and sefer torah
Beis Shammai's restriction: only ochel nefesh (food purposes) allowed
Beis Hillel's permissive approach and the question of scope

macro_segments.title: The Shechitah of Nedavah: Moving Beyond Carrying to a General Principle

micro_segments.title: The Baraita: shechting a free-will olah on Yom Tov incurs lashes
Rabbi Yitzchak bar Abdimi identifies this as a Beis Shammai position
Inference for Beis Hillel: mitoch principle—once permitted for purpose, permitted without purpose

macro_segments.title: Rabbah's Challenge: Is the Mitoch Debate Limited to Carrying or General to All Melachos?

micro_segments.title: Rabbah asks whether the Beis Shammai–Beis Hillel dispute applies to all melachos or only to carrying
Distinction between Shabbat and Yom Tov regarding carrying: eruv ve-hotza'ah
Beis Shammai holds carrying is forbidden on Yom Tov; Beis Hillel holds it is not prohibited
Biblical source: the verse in Jeremiah distinguishes Shabbat from Yom Tov

macro_segments.title: Why Carrying Is Different: Melacha Gru'ah and the Structural Distinction from Other Melachos

micro_segments.title: Rashi's explanation: carrying is not fundamentally in the prohibition of lo ta'aseh kol melacha
Carrying as melacha gru'ah (deficient melacha): non-creative, lacking the productive character of other melachos
Carrying's role in commerce and business: distinct from the creative melachos
Carrying in the context of Yom Tov allowances: essential to food procurement (shopping)

macro_segments.title: Rambam's Framework: Melechet Avodah vs. Ochel Nefesh—Two Approaches to Yom Tov Restrictions

micro_segments.title: The two biblical formulations: ach asher ye'achel lechol nefesh vs. kol meleches avodah lo ta'asu
Ochel nefesh approach: case-by-case justification required for each act
Melechet avodah approach: certain melachos categorically excluded as non-laborious

macro_segments.title: The Scope of Mitoch: Which Melachos Qualify and What Justification Is Needed?

micro_segments.title: Tosafot distinguishes: mitoch not applied to other melachos like building (e.g., making cheese)

macro_segments.title: Tosafot's Nuanced Reading: Mitoch Requires Justification but Applies Broadly to Food-Related Acts

micro_segments.title: Tosafot disputes Rashi's infinite allowance and argues mitoch still requires some tzorech
Rabbeinu Chananel's strict reading: tzorech must be a mitzvah purpose
Tosafot's broader standard: any purpose that fits Yom Tov enjoyment (simcha) suffices
Practical example: playing ball on Yom Tov in public demonstrates the broad license

macro_segments.title: Rabbi Yochanan's Extension of Mitoch: Cooking and Fire as Non-Restricted Melachos

micro_segments.title: Rabbi Yochanan holds that the mitoch principle applies to melachos beyond carrying
The Baraita of cooking gid ha-nasheh with milk on Yom Tov: five distinct lashes
Rabbi Yochanan rejects the baraita: cooking and fire are included in Beis Hillel's mitoch allowance

macro_segments.title: The Scope of Mitoch: Which Melachos Qualify and What Justification Is Needed? (continued)

micro_segments.title: Gemara confirms mitoch applies to carrying, shechitah, fire, and cooking—the food-essential melachos
Two remaining questions: how broad is the justification threshold, and does every melacha allow mitoch?
Practical implication: the Yerushalmi's case of ner shel batalah (lighting a candle without purpose)

macro_segments.title: Shift from Biblical Principles to Derabbanan: Delivering Challah and Matanot to the Kohen on Yom Tov

micro_segments.title: The Mishnah: Beis Shammai forbids delivering challah and matanot to the kohen on Yom Tov
The allowance to bake bread and shecht animals on Yom Tov creates a need to separate challah and matanot
The concern: allowing delivery of gifts could lead to separating terumah, which is forbidden
Beis Hillel's argument: gifts are permitted to separate, so delivery should be allowed too

macro_segments.title: Three Baraita Versions: Mapping the Dispute Between Beis Shammai and Beis Hillel

micro_segments.title: Version 1 (Rabbi Yehuda): Beis Shammai concedes if shechted/baked today; debate only on yesterdays items
Version 2 (Rabbi Yosi): Both agree gifts are deliverable; debate only on terumah
Version 3 (Acherim): Beis Shammai forbids delivery in all cases; Beis Hillel permits

macro_segments.title: Rava's Resolution: The Mishnah Refers to Separation on Yom Tov but Shechitah Yesterday

micro_segments.title: Rava clarifies: the Mishnah discusses cases where separation occurred on Yom Tov
Even Acherim (Version 3) concedes Beis Shammai allows delivery if shechted today
The distinction between versions: Rabbi Yehuda adds the idea of bundling yesterday's items with today's

macro_segments.title: Halacha Ruling: Following Rabbi Yosi—Gifts Are Permitted on Yom Tov, Only Terumah Debated

micro_segments.title: Rav Yehuda in the name of Shmuel: the halacha follows Rabbi Yosi
Practical case: Tuvi son of Rav Nechemiah seeks permission to give wine-terumah to a kohen on Yom Tov
Rabbi Yosi's ruling: both Beis Shammai and Beis Hillel permit delivery of terumah on Yom Tov

topical_tags.term: Mitoch (Once Permitted for Purpose, Permitted Without Purpose)
